rationale: The page provides detailed information on black hole mergers, the characteristics and formation of black holes, stellar evolution pathways, and gravitational waves. It includes specific scientific terms, historical context (Einstein, Nobel Prize), and diagrams explaining physical processes, making it highly suitable for generating technical questions.
cleanedText:
3. DEVELOPMENT RELATED WITH PHYSICS
3.1. BLACK HOLE MERGER
Why in the News?
An international network of gravitational wave observatories has recently detected the merger of two exceptionally massive black holes.
More on the News
- Two black holes merged to form a black hole estimated to be about 225 times the size of the Sun. This is the biggest black hole merger ever observed.
- The detection of the gravitational waves (assigned name is GW231123) from this event was made by the LVK network of observatories, involving the LIGO detector in the United States, Virgo in Italy and KAGRA in Japan.
Black Holes
- A place in space where gravity pulls so much that even light cannot get out. The gravity is so strong because matter has been squeezed into a tiny space.
- Black holes don’t emit or reflect light, making them effectively invisible to telescopes. However, scientists can see how the strong gravity affects the stars and gas around the black hole.
- Formation of Black Hole: Most black holes are formed from the remnants of a large star that dies in a supernova explosion. When stars are especially large, the core collapses into a black hole. Otherwise, the core becomes an ultra-dense neutron star.
- Types of Black Holes:
- Stellar-Mass Black Holes
- Intermediate-Mass Black Holes (IMBH)
- Supermassive Black Holes
Stellar Evolution
- Low- and Medium-Mass Stars (including the Sun): Nebula -> Main Sequence -> Red Giant -> "Planetary" Nebula -> White Dwarf.
- High-Mass Stars: Nebula -> Main Sequence -> Red Supergiant -> Supernova -> Neutron Star.
- Very High-Mass Star: Nebula -> Main Sequence -> Red Supergiant -> Supernova -> Black Hole.
Gravitational Waves
- These are 'ripples' in space-time caused by some of the most violent and energetic processes in the Universe.
- Predicted in Einstein's General Theory of Relativity.
- Travel at the speed of light.
- They permeate the universe at high frequency creating a cosmic hum.
- GWs’ first detection: Detected at the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory (LIGO) in 2015.
- 2017 Nobel Prize in Physics was awarded "for decisive contributions to the LIGO detector and the observation of gravitational waves".
Events Responsible for Gravitational Wave
- When a star explodes asymmetrically (called a supernova).
- When two big stars orbit each other.
- When two black holes orbit each other and merge.
- Black hole-neutron star merger.
